subject: Chemistry
title: Argon: The Lazy Gas
description: Learn Argon: The Lazy Gas in one minute.

hook.text: About 1% of every breath you take is argon—a gas that does absolutely nothing.

buildup.text: Lord Rayleigh noticed that nitrogen from air was heavier than nitrogen made in the lab. Something else was hiding in air.

discovery.text: In 1894, he and William Ramsay isolated a new gas. They named it argon—Greek for 'lazy' or 'inactive.'

twist.text: Argon has a full outer electron shell. It has no reason to bond with anything—ever.

climax.text: We pump argon into light bulbs and welding torches. Its laziness protects hot metals from reacting.

punchline.text: Named 'lazy' because it reacts with nothing. Perfectly useless, perfectly useful.

quiz.question: Why is argon called 'the lazy gas'?
options: It doesn't react with any other elements
It moves very slowly through air
It was the last noble gas to be discovered
